464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# SOP Audit: The Forge Belfast

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member Onboarding** – no documented first-touch flow (assessment → goal-setting → intro class → first month check-ins). Critical for retention baseline.
- **Cancellation & Churn** – no win-back or exit interview process; you're losing data on why members leave.
- **Member Checkins** – no scheduled touchpoint cadence (30/60/90 day retention calls/texts). Retention dies without this.
- **Coaching Development Roadmap** – no career progression or skill certification path; coaches stall, churn increases.
- **Class Quality Audit Checklist** – no standardized observation/feedback mechanism; inconsistent member experience across class times.
- **GDPR & Member Data Privacy** – critical compliance gap; email marketing, contact storage, consent logs undefended.
- **New Quarter Program Launch** – no repeatable go-live playbook (comms calendar, coach briefing, marketing calendar sync).
- **Referral Programme** – no documented mechanics, incentive rules, or tracking; leaving revenue on table.
- **Pause/Cancellation Pause SOP** – no freeze protocol; ad-hoc handling likely bleeding members mid-pause.
- **Supply Chain/Order Supplies & Sundries** – no reorder triggers, vendor contacts, or inventory minimum thresholds.
- **Incident & Complaints** – no formal log, escalation, or resolution SOP; legal/reputation risk.
- **Health & Safety Incident Response** – no protocol for injuries, near-misses, or safeguarding concerns.

**Needs updating**

- **Coaches SOPs (20/24)** – likely missing: Weekly Key Coach Tasks, coaching cues standardization, sub/cover protocol, how to handle difficult member dynamics.

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing is under-deployed.** AR/M has collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June) — a 29% drop despite headcount rising 18 members. You've grown the studio *and* diluted unit revenue. Implement tiered membership tiers (Basic £25/month, Premium £35 with class booking priority, Elite £50 with PT credits) to re-segment 147 members and recover to £155+ AR/M by end of Q3. This alone adds £5k+/month at no volume cost.

• **Retention collapsed Q2 and remains poor.** January–April showed 5.74–8.33% churn; June hit 0% (suspicious or data gap?). But May–June member growth was only +19 despite a doubled studio — you're leaking members faster than new intake fills seats. Introduce a 90-day onboarding program, exit interview follow-ups for churned members, and a referral bonus (£15 credit for each friend who signs up) to hit 3% churn max. At 147 members, 3% churn vs 5.74% saves ~14 members/month = £1,800+ retained revenue.

• **Non-peak class slots are empty.** A doubled studio means fixed costs scale but utilisation must follow. Run a 2-week attendance audit by class/time slot (early morning, midday, evening). Offer "Quiet Hour" discounts (50% off) or free drop-in taster sessions at 9am or 2pm weekdays. Target: fill 70% of all class slots; currently you're likely at 50–55%.

• **Upsell PT and retail hard.** With 147 members, even 10% uptake of 1×PT session/month at £40 = £588/month incremental. Stock branded merch (water bottles, gym wear, protein) and offer members 20% discount; typical margin 40% on £500/month retail turnover = £200 profit. Train all staff to pitch PT in induction and post-class.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people expenses are the crisis.** June hit £9,390 (52.9% of revenue); May was £9,233. Excluding April's relocation, this is 52–53% of turnover vs 28–32% in Sep–Nov. Drill into the detail: rent for the new studio is likely higher, but utilities, supplies, and contractor costs need forensic review. Request itemised P&

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: Her Build: Lower (17 sessions), Build: Upper (9), Elevate (6), and Strongevity (3) must move to JoJo and Eilis. Eilis should take Build: Full Body and Elevate slots she's already confident with; JoJo takes Build: Lower and Strongevity to round out her week outside Tuesdays.

• **JoJo is significantly over-loaded at 133 sessions**: Even after Grace's classes shift, prioritise reducing her PT load (44 sessions is high). Review which clients truly need JoJo's expertise vs. Eilis, and transition 8–12 PT slots to Eilis to free JoJo's calendar and prevent burnout.

• **Cover Grace's No Sweat Intro sessions (3) with a rotating pair approach**: Pair JoJo or Eilis with a senior member or admin staff to run these low-intensity onboarding sessions, so they don't fall on coaching time alone.

• **Eilis has capacity and should own Elevate consistently**: She's run 7 Elevate sessions and shown capability; assign her a regular Elevate slot to build member relationships and reduce fragmentation across the timetable.

• **Don't let Body Composition Scans inflate coaching load**: Reassign Grace's 9 scans and JoJo's 20 scans to Eilis or a non-coaching staff member where possible—they're admin, not coaching time, and are masking true PT/class demand.

• **Audit PT demand vs. coach skill fit**: Eilis has only 11 PT sessions. If demand exists, grow her PT book and vet which clients genuinely need JoJo's head-coach expertise; this is the fastest way to rebalance and let Grace step out

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. **Add a paid "Open Strength Lab" slot** on Thursday or Monday mornings (your quietest days) — 45 mins of unsupervised barbell access for members who want flexible training outside class times. This fills dead air and appeals to Build-class enthusiasts wanting extra volume.

2. **Run a monthly "Intro to Barbelle" workshop** (30 mins, small group, £5 add-on) in a quiet Tuesday or Wednesday afternoon slot. Barbelle's 27 attendees suggests intimidation or unclear positioning — a one-off walkthrough removes friction and could convert curious members.

3. **Block out Saturday 10am–11am as "Coach's Office Hours"** — members book 15-min PT consultations, form checks, or programme reviews at £15 each. Uses dead Saturday time productively and creates a revenue stream from quiet periods.

**For the quieter classes themselves:**

- **Move Engine or Capacity to Wednesday evening** (your busiest day) when gym energy is high — timing and atmosphere directly affect energy-based classes more than strength ones.
- **Rebrand Strongevity and Games Night** with member testimonials (age, fitness gains) — these sound gimmicky. Reframe as "Strength for 40+" and "Competitive conditioning" and email

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution — The Forge

**1. Audit what you're ACTUALLY selling right now**
Walk your gym floor. Interview 3-5 coaches separately: "What do members ask for? What do you wish was clearer?" You'll find gaps between what you think you offer and what's actually happening. This is your real menu, not an invented one.

**2. Price anchor from your highest-value offering first**
Start with your most premium service (1-on-1 coaching? small group? membership tier?). Price that, then work backwards. Members compare UP, not down. If your premium offering is weak, everything else looks cheap.

**3. Create TWO documents, not one integrated menu**
**Coach version:** What they're authorized to sell, commission structure, upsell triggers ("member has done 8 sessions → suggest PT package")
**Member version:** What the tiers actually get (response time on form, priority booking, etc.) — be stupidly specific. "Unlimited gym access" vs. "Gym access Mon-Fri 6am-10pm, weekends 8am-6pm" changes perception.

**4. Test the menu with your most demanding member first**
Share draft with someone who asks lots of questions. If she can't explain it to a friend in 30 seconds, it's not clear. Rewrite until it is.

**5. Document the process, not just the prices**
Add a "How to access [service]" flowchart for coaches. E.g., "Member wants PT → Coach uses form X → Books slot in system Y → Sends Grace the signed agreement." Prevents chaos when demand hits.

**Done in a week?** Yes —

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

Your CPL has inflated 4.5x in four months (£6 to £27) while absolute lead volume has collapsed 94% (69 to 4). This isn't a gradual decline—it's a cliff edge in July, suggesting either creative fatigue, audience saturation, or a hard campaign pause/technical issue. Your close rate has held strong at 75%, so the problem isn't messaging-to-conversion; it's reach and resonance dying. At £27/lead with a £567-per-customer revenue, you're still marginally profitable (75% × £567 = £425 customer value), but you're operating at the edge. You've also dropped spend to £109 in July—whether deliberate or accidental, this signals either budget starvation or a loss of confidence in the channel.

**Actions**

1. **Audit and reactivate immediately.** Check if the July campaign was paused, budget-capped, or running against a broken pixel. Pull Meta's Ads Manager diagnostics on link clicks, impressions, and frequency. If the campaign was live, frequency is likely >3.0 (audience burnout); if it was off, turn it back on at July 2024's £417 spend level and monitor 7-day performance.

2. **Refresh creative hard.** Run three entirely new video/image ad variants next week—test one focused on "women-only safe space" (emotional/community angle, not just fitness), one on "6-week transformation stories" (social proof from April's 20 conversions), and one on a new pain point (e.g. "no intimidation, no mansplaining"). Pause your top April/May performers; they're exhausted.

3. **Narrow and warm your audience.** Your April audience was broad; your July was likely too saturated or too cold. Segment: (a) lookalike audience built from your 23 May conversions only, (b) custom audience of website visitors in last 90 days who didn't convert, (c) interest-based targeting on "women's fitness," "gym," "wellness" in Belfast postcodes BT2–BT17 only (tighter geo). Ditch broad reach;

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTIONABLE BRIEF

## REVENUE — PRIORITY 1: ARREST THE CHURN SPIKE & REV-PER-MEMBER DECLINE

**Last 3 months (Apr–Jun 2026) vs prior 3 (Jan–Mar 2026):**
- Revenue flat: £15.5k avg vs £14.5k avg (+6.9%, modest)
- **Active members +24 (121 → 145), but Rev/member DOWN £13 (£130 → £109). You're acquiring members at lower value or lower engagement.**
- **May churn spiked to 11.9%** (vs 5.1% in Jan); June recovered to 3.2% — suggests onboarding issue post-relocation or pricing/comms misstep.

**Immediate moves:**
- **Audit May cohort**: who churned post-April relocation? New member experience poor, or class schedule/timetable disruption? Fix onboarding + welcome series.
- **Upsell PT & classes à la carte**: Rev/member is sliding despite headcount growth — members are on base tier. At 145 members, offer 1-to-1 sessions or class packs as £20–40/month bolt-ons. Target 30% take-up = +£870/month revenue.
- **Retention target**: get churn back to 3.5% (April level). At 145 members, 11.9% churn = 17 lost per month; drop to 3.5% = 5 lost, net +12 retained per month × £130/member = +£1,560/month run-rate.

---

## EXPENSES — PROBLEM: MARCH & APRIL SPIKE; ONGOING PAYROLL CREEP

**Last 3 months cash out: £20.6k (Jun), £39.0k (Apr, relocation excluded), £23.3k (Mar avg ~£21k).**
- **March & June: £23.3k & £20.6k against £15.7k revenue = 147% & 131% cost ratio.** (vs Feb 16.5k spend on £15.5k = healthy.)
- Relocation (Apr) excluded per your note, but March overspend (£23.3k) wasn't relocation — that's a red flag.

**Moves:**
- **Itemise March outflow**: supplier bills, wages, utilities spike? Likely one-off (rates, insurance renewal, or stock purchase), but verify — if recurring,
